resume:
Junior Instructional Designer — 2 years building digital learning content.

BrightPath Learning (2023-present): Instructional Designer I. Built self-paced e-learning modules in Articulate Storyline for corporate compliance training. Resume summary says 'led the full redesign of the onboarding curriculum'; experience bullets later say 'supported the senior designer on the onboarding refresh.'

Campus Tutoring Center (2021-2023, part-time during school): Peer tutor and workshop facilitator. Header lists '3 years' of facilitation; dates only cover ~2 years.

Projects: converted a 4-hour instructor-led workshop into a 90-minute online module; built knowledge-check quizzes.

Education: BA Education, minor in Communications (2023).

Tools: Articulate Storyline, Rise, Camtasia, basic HTML/CSS, Google Workspace.
jd: We are hiring a Junior Instructional Designer to join our learning-experience team. You will build self-paced e-learning modules, write assessment items aligned to learning objectives, and collaborate with subject-matter experts to translate content into engaging interactions. Day-to-day responsibilities include storyboarding, developing in Articulate Storyline and Rise, running usability checks with a small learner sample, and iterating based on completion and quiz-performance data. We look for candidates who can clearly describe their own contribution on a project, articulate the learning problem they were solving, and show how they measured whether the learning actually landed. Comfort taking SME feedback and a growth mindset matter more than years of experience.

history.q: Tell me about your role at BrightPath and what you build day to day.
Your summary says you led the onboarding redesign, but the bullets say you supported it. Which was it?
Okay — on that onboarding project, what was the actual learning problem you were trying to fix?
Who decided it needed a redesign, and how was that decision made?
Walk me through the workshop-to-online conversion. What did you change and why?
How did you decide what to cut to get from 4 hours down to 90 minutes?
What tools did you actually author it in?
How did you write the knowledge-check questions — what were they aligned to?
Did you test the module with any real learners before launch?
Tell me about a time an SME pushed back on your design. What happened?
What's one thing you'd do differently on that conversion project?
Give me the full story of one module you're proud of: the problem, what you did, and how you knew it worked.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. So the compliance module had a real problem — completion rates were sitting around 60% and people were clearly skimming, because the old version was just a 30-slide wall of text with a quiz at the end. So I rebuilt it: I storyboarded a scenario-based flow where learners make decisions as a new employee, added branching with feedback, and broke it into three short chapters with a check after each one. I authored the whole thing in Storyline and pulled in the SME twice to validate the scenarios. Honestly I was really proud of how it turned out — it felt much more engaging and the SME loved the branching.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: it felt much more engaging and the SME loved the branching

expected-yield: the missing Result of the STAR story — the post-launch completion rate or quiz-score change versus the 60% baseline, and over what period, rather than a subjective claim that it felt more engaging
